comments.text: If I understand correctly, the way this is formalised in Lean requires the multiset $A$ to have the property that the set $$P(A) = \left \{\sum_{n \in B} B \subseteq A \text{ finite} \right\}$$ contains all positive integers, and a lot of texts define completeness this way. In the book by Graham and Erdős however, it is only required that $P(A)$ contains all sufficiently large integers, which is also often taken as a definition of completeness. For what it is worth, with the former interpretation that is used in the Lean formalisation I can fully settle this problem, and show that no such multiset exists for any $m, n$ with $2 \le m < n$. Assume $A = \{a_1, a_2 \ldots, \}$ is such that $P(A \setminus \{a_i, a_j \}) = \mathbb{N}$ holds for all indices $i, j$. Set $s_1 = a_1$ and, for any $j \ge 2$, $s_j = a_i$ where $i$ is such that $a_{i-1} > s_1 + \ldots + s_{j-1}$. With the infinite set $S$ equal to $\{s_1, s_2, \ldots \}$, one can then check that $P(A \setminus S) = \mathbb{N}$ holds as well. For a complete write-up, see here. Perhaps the argument can be adapted to settle the second (intended) interpretation as well. EDIT: Come to think of it, I don't think this argument can be easily adapted to the intended interpreation, because the sequence $a_n = F_n + (-1)^n$ with $F_n$ the $n$-th Fibonacci number has the property that $P(A \setminus \{a_i, a_j\})$ contains all large enough integers for all $i, j$, but no infinite subset $S$ exists for which $P(A \setminus S)$ contains all large enough integers. See #346 and references for more information. EDIT 25-09-2025: I have left a comment on the Lean forum and the formalisation has now been updated to reflect the intended meaning. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
